final_message: Hello there! I am Pal, your friendly math tutor. ✨ Today we are going to learn about something magical called reflection symmetry. Imagine folding a piece of paper in half. That fold is called a line of symmetry because it divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry that goes straight up and down, making the left and right sides match perfectly. The letter $$A$$ is a great example of this! Other shapes have a horizontal line going side to side, and some special letters like $$H$$ have both. You can even use this idea to make beautiful paper cutouts by drawing just half of a shape on the folded edge and opening it up. Are you ready to explore some mirror images together? 🪞

orchestrator_intent.goal: Introduce the concept of reflection symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The session is just starting and the section status is not_started, so the dialogue agent should introduce the new concept.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.

student_message: One horizontal line! I win! Next!
final_message:
You definitely win the reflection symmetry game! 🏆 Since you are zooming through, let's jump right into our brand new chapter like you asked. 

Today we are learning about rotational symmetry. Imagine spinning a shape around its very center. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being turned by a fraction of a full circle, like a $$1/4$$ turn, a $$1/2$$ turn, or a $$3/4$$ turn. If it only looks the same after spinning all the way around in a full turn, it doesn't count! A fun trick is to draw a little dot on the top of your shape and watch where the dot goes as you turn it. 

Are you ready to try spinning some shapes with me? 🌀

orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is highly confident, focused, and explicitly asking for the next chapter. Since the rotational symmetry section is not_started, the dialogue agent should introduce the new concept.

You can track this by placing a dot on the shape and observing where the dot ends up after each turn.

final_message:
Boom! You totally win! 🏆 You have officially mastered turns and lines. Since you are zooming through this, it is time to use your superpowers to start making symmetrical designs! 

You can deliberately create amazing patterns that have reflection symmetry, rotational symmetry, or even both. Remember, a design has rotational symmetry if it looks the exact same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if an invisible mirror line splits it into two perfectly matching halves. By carefully arranging or coloring shapes, like squares and triangles, you can build super cool designs with totally different symmetry powers. 

Are you ready to arrange some shapes and create your very own symmetrical masterpiece? 🎨

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.

final_message:
You got it! A $$1/4$$ turn is perfect! 🚀 Since you are such a symmetry master, let's zoom right into our next exciting chapter. 

Have you ever heard of block printing? It is a beautiful traditional craft from Rajasthan. Artisans carve a cool design into a wooden block and press it onto fabric over and over to make amazing repeating patterns. When they stamp the same block in different ways, the final design can have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or even both! The really tricky part is that the print on the fabric is actually a mirror image of the carved block because it gets pressed face-down. 

Are you ready to use your super speed to explore these mirror images? 🎨

current_section_content: Block printing is a traditional craft from Rajasthan where artisans carve a design into a wooden block and press it repeatedly onto fabric to create beautiful repeating patterns. When the same block is stamped multiple times in different orientations, the final printed design can have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both. Importantly, the print produced by a block is the mirror image of the carved block, because the block is pressed face-down. By analysing how a block is repeated and rotated, you can predict and identify the symmetry of the resulting pattern.
